Idiomatic Expressions

  • 婕妤益贵幸，号昭仪 - Indicates a rise in status and honor, with 'Jieyu' becoming increasingly favored and being honored with the title of 'Zhaoyi'.
  • 求近远条馆 - Suggests a desire for proximity to a specific location, in this case, the 'distant Tao Hall'.
  • 帝作少嫔馆，为露华殿、含风殿、博昌殿、求安殿 - Describes the construction of various halls and pavilions, including 'Luohua', 'Hanfeng', 'Bocang', and 'Qiuan' pavilions.
  • 又为温室、凝室、浴兰室，曲房连槛 - Further details the luxurious living quarters, including 'warm rooms', 'cool rooms', and 'baths where lilies were cultivated', with 'winding rooms connected by corridors'.
  • 饰黄金、白玉，以璧为表里，千变万状 - Emphasizes the opulence and variety of decorations, using 'gold', 'jade', and 'jade screens' on the inside and out, taking on 'myriad forms'.
  • 连远条馆，号通仙门 - Highlights the connection between the new structures and the 'distant Tao Hall', known as the 'Through-the-Heavenly Gate'.
  • 后贵宠，益思放荡 - Suggests a shift towards a more unrestrained lifestyle, with the empress, favored and pampered, beginning to think more about indulgence.
  • 使人博求术士，求匪安却老之方 - Indicates a search for longevity and immortality, with the empress sending people to widely seek out sages and methods to avoid aging and death.
  • 时西南比波夷致贡，其使者举茹一饭，昼夜不卧偃 - Describes the unusual abilities of the envoy from the southwestern tribe, who could eat a meal while standing and did not sleep or lie down day or night.
  • 典属国上其状，屡有光怪 - Reports the strange phenomena associated with the envoy, noting frequent 'strange phenomena'.
  • 后闻之，问何如术 - Shows the empress's curiosity about the envoy's skills, asking what kind of skill he possessed.
  • 夷人曰：“吾术天地平，生死齐，出入有无，变化万象，而卒不化。” - Explains the nature of the envoy's skills, stating that his skill makes 'heaven and earth level', 'life and death equal', and he can 'appear and disappear, transform into myriad forms, yet ultimately remain unchanged'.
  • 后令樊弟子不周遗千金 - Shows the empress's generosity and interest in the skill, ordering Fan's disciple, Buzhou, to give him a thousand gold coins.
  • 夷人曰：“学吾术者，要不淫与谩言。” - Sets conditions for learning the skill, stating that those who learn it must not indulge in lust or speak falsely.
  • 后遂不报 - Indicates a change in the empress's attitude, as she then did not respond.
  • 他日，樊侍后浴，语甚欢 - Describes a moment of intimacy and joy, with Fan serving the empress in her bath and they speaking very happily.
  • 后为樊道夷言，抵掌笑曰：“忆在江都时，阳华李姑，畜斗鸭水池上，苦獭啮鸭。时下朱里芮姥者，求捕獭狸献。姥谓姑曰：‘是狸不他食，当饭以鸭。’姑怒，绞其狸。今夷术，真似此也。” - Is a humorous anecdote comparing the envoy's skill to the otter's diet, with the empress recalling a story about fighting ducks in a pond and an otter eating them.
  • 后大笑曰：“臭夷何足污我绞乎！” - Shows the empress's dismissive attitude towards the envoy, laughing loudly and saying that the envoy is not worth tainting her scissors.
  • 后所通官奴燕赤凤者，雄捷能超观阁，兼通昭仪 - Describes Yan Chifeng's abilities and role, noting that he is agile and can leap over the observatory, and he also serves the Zhaoyi.
  • 赤凤始出少嫔馆，后适来幸 - Describes a coincidental encounter, with Yan Chifeng first leaving the lesser concubine's hall and the empress happening to come by.

  • 抽紫玉九雛釵 - Refers to pulling out a purple jade hairpin with nine chicks.

  • 以漢家火德 - The Han dynasty's association with the element of fire, which was believed to influence the character and fate of the dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 私婢不豫约中 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'a private maid not included in the agreement' or 'a private maid who was not forewarned.'
  • 出帏值帝 - Literally 'came out of the curtain and met the emperor,' likely referring to a maid unexpectedly encountering the emperor.
  • 遽隐避 - Means 'quickly hid away,' indicating a sudden and secretive action.
  • 从兰室帏中窥昭仪 - Literally 'from the purple chamber curtain peeped at the concubine,' suggesting the emperor secretly watched Empress Zhao.
  • 多袖金 - Means 'carried many gold pieces in his sleeves,' indicating the emperor brought gifts.
  • 逢侍儿私婢，辄牵止赐之 - Means 'whenever he met a maid or private servant, he would pull them aside and give them gifts.'
  • 侍儿贪帝金，一出一人不绝 - Means 'the maids were greedy for the emperor's gold, and one after another kept coming out.'
  • 使夜从帑益至百余金 - Means 'he sent someone at night to bring more gold, up to a hundred gold pieces.'
  • 帝病缓弱，太医万方不能救 - Means 'the emperor was ill and weak, and the imperial physicians could not cure him in any way.'
  • 求奇药，尝得胶，遗昭仪 - Means 'he sought a miraculous medicine, and once obtained some glue, he sent it to Empress Zhao.'
  • 辄进帝，一丸一幸 - Means 'she would give it to the emperor, and each pill would result in an encounter.'
  • 一夕，昭仪醉进七丸 - Means 'one night, Empress Zhao, drunk, gave him seven pills.'
  • 帝昏夜拥昭仪居九成帐，笑吃吃不绝 - Means 'the emperor, in the dark, embraced Empress Zhao in the nine-tiered canopy, laughing continuously.'
  • 抵明，帝起御衣，阴精流输不禁 - Means 'by dawn, the emperor got up wearing his imperial robes, and his semen flowed uncontrollably.'
  • 有顷绝倒，衣视帝，余精出涌，沾污被内 - Means 'soon he collapsed, and the maids saw the emperor, with semen flowing out and staining the bedding.'
  • 须臾帝崩 - Means 'soon the emperor died.'
  • 宫人以白太后 - Means 'the palace women reported this to the empress dowager.'
  • 太后使理昭仪 - Means 'the empress dowager ordered Empress Zhao to be dealt with.'
  • 吾持人主如婴儿，宠倾天下，安能敛手掖庭令，争帷帐之事乎 - Means 'I held the emperor like a baby, with my favor overwhelming the world, how could I submit to the palace women and fight over matters in the bedchamber?'
  • 乃拊膺呼曰：“帝何往乎！”遂呕血而死 - Means 'she slapped her chest and cried, 'Where has the emperor gone?' and then vomited blood and died.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 私婢 - Refers to a private maid, a low-ranking servant in the imperial household.
  • 昭仪 - A high-ranking concubine in the imperial harem, equivalent to a secondary empress.
  • 九成帐 - A luxurious and elaborate canopy, symbolizing the emperor's wealth and status.
  • 太医 - Imperial physicians, highly skilled doctors who served the emperor.
  • 奇药 - Miraculous medicine, often associated with elixirs of immortality.
  • 胶 - Glue, which in this context might be a metaphor for a substance that causes impotence or other effects.
  • 宫人 - Palace women, including maids and concubines.
  • 太后 - Empress dowager, the mother of the emperor or his predecessor.
  • 掖庭令 - The official in charge of the imperial harem, responsible for managing the concubines and maids.

  • 奇药 - Miraculous medicine.
  • 胶 - Glue.
  • 宫人 - Palace women.
  • 太后 - Empress dowager.

  • 善踽步行 - Refers to the ability to walk gracefully, like someone holding a flower branch and swaying gently.
  • 若人手执花枝，颤颤然 - A simile describing the graceful and delicate movement, as if holding a flower branch and swaying gently.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汝之姊 - Direct address meaning 'your sister' in Classical Chinese.
  • 吾欲枭其首，断其手足，置干溷中 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'I want to decapitate her, cut off her hands and feet, and place them in the latrine,' conveying extreme anger or vengeance.
  • 乃快吾意 - Phrase indicating the speaker's emotional state, meaning 'to satisfy my desire.'
  • 何缘而得罪 - Question seeking the reason for a perceived wrong, meaning 'why did she offend?'
  • 壁衣中事 - Euphemism for a scandal or secret, possibly referring to something hidden in the wall or a metaphorical 'wall of silence.'
  • 臣妾缘后得备后宫 - Statement of loyalty and gratitude, meaning 'I, as a concubine, owe my position in the harem to the Empress.'
  • 后死则妾安能独生 - Rhetorical expression of solidarity, meaning 'if the Empress dies, how could I survive alone?'
  • 陛下无故而杀一后 - Critique of the Emperor's actions, meaning 'the Emperor kills an Empress without cause.'
  • 天下有以窥陛下也 - Implication that the act would reveal the Emperor's character, meaning 'the world will see the Emperor's true nature.'
  • 愿得身实鼎镬，体膏斧钺 - Dramatic expression of willingness to die for the Empress, meaning 'I wish to be put to death in a cauldron or smeared with the blood of an execution.'
  • 因大恸，以身投地 - Physical expression of grief, meaning 'she weeps bitterly and throws herself to the ground.'
  • 吾以汝故，因不害后 - Statement of restraint due to the speaker's influence, meaning 'I did not harm her because of you.'
  • 脱或再有过 - Conditional statement about future actions, meaning 'if there is another mistake.'
  • 事不可救，身首异地，为天下笑 - Dramatic expression of despair, meaning 'if the situation cannot be saved, I will be separated from my body and laughed at by the world.'
  • 今日妾能拯救也 - Statement of intervention, meaning 'today, I can save you.'
  • 存殁无定 - Reflection on the unpredictability of fate, meaning 'life and death are uncertain.'
  • 或亦妾死 - Statement of potential sacrifice, meaning 'or perhaps I will die.'
  • 姊尚谁攀乎 - Rhetorical question about the sister's future, meaning 'who will you cling to then?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 壁衣中事 - Euphemism for a scandal or secret, possibly referring to something hidden in the wall or a metaphorical 'wall of silence.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昭仪方浴，帝私视 - 昭仪正在沐浴，皇帝偷偷观看。‘私视’意味着皇帝偷偷观看，带有私密和窥探的意味。
  • 侍者报昭仪，昭仪急趋烛后避 - 侍女报告昭仪，昭仪急忙躲到烛后。‘趋’表示快速移动，‘烛后’指烛火后面，意味着昭仪迅速躲藏。
  • 帝瞥见之，心愈眩惑 - 皇帝瞥见了她，心中更加迷惑。‘瞥见’表示短暂地看到，‘眩惑’表示困惑和迷惑。
  • 帝常语近侍曰 - 皇帝常常对近侍说。‘常语’表示经常说的话。
  • 自古人主无二后，若有则吾立昭仪为后矣 - 自古以来，君主只有一个皇后，如果有两个，我就立昭仪为皇后。‘无二后’表示没有两个皇后。
  • 赵后知帝见昭仪益加宠幸，乃具汤浴，请帝以观 - 赵皇后知道皇帝对昭仪更加宠爱，于是准备了汤浴，请皇帝来看。‘具汤浴’表示准备了热水澡。
  • 后体以水沃，愈亲近，而帝愈不乐，不终幸而去 - 赵皇后用水浇洗身体，更加亲近，但皇帝却越来越不高兴，没有完成幸事就离开了。‘沃’表示浇洗，‘不终幸而去’表示没有完成幸事就离开了。
  • 后泣曰 - 赵皇后哭泣说。‘泣’表示哭泣。
  • 爱在一身，无可奈何 - 爱在一个人身上，无可奈何。表达了赵皇后对皇帝的无奈。
  • 后生日，昭仪为贺，帝亦同往 - 赵皇后的生日，昭仪前来祝贺，皇帝也一同前往。‘为贺’表示前来祝贺。
  • 酒半酣，后欲感动帝意，乃泣数行 - 酒喝到一半，赵皇后想感动皇帝的心意，于是流下了几行眼泪。‘酒半酣’表示酒喝到一半，‘泣数行’表示流下了几行眼泪。
  • 不数日，备后宫 - 没过几天，昭仪就准备好了后宫。‘备后宫’表示准备好了后宫，意味着昭仪的地位得到了提升。

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 昭仪 - 昭仪是中国古代后宫中的一种妃嫔称号，地位仅次于皇后。

  • 经三月，乃诈托有孕 - Narrative expression meaning 'after three months, she falsely claimed to be pregnant.'
  • 上笺奏 - Formal expression meaning 'she submitted a written report.'
  • 云：“臣妾久备掖庭，先承幸御，遣肆大号，积有岁时。近因始生之日，复加善祝之私，时屈乘舆，俯临东掖，久侍宴私，再承幸御。臣妾数月来，内宫盈实，月脉不流。饮食美甘，不异常日。知圣躬在体，辨天日之入怀。虹初贯日，总是珍符，龙已据胸，兹为佳瑞。更期蕃育圣嗣，抱日趋庭，瞻望圣明。踊跃临贺，谨以此闻。” - Long, formal report in which the concubine claims to be pregnant, using elaborate and metaphorical language to describe her supposed condition.
  • 帝时在西宫，得奏，喜动颜色 - Narrative expression meaning 'the emperor, who was at the Western Palace, received the report and his face lit up with joy.'
  • 答云：“因阅来奏，喜庆交集。夫妻之私，义均一体。社稷之重，嗣续其先。好体方初，保绥宜厚。药有性者勿举，食无毒者可亲。有恳求上，元烦笺奏，口授宫使可矣。” - Formal response from the emperor, expressing his joy and giving instructions for the concubine's care.
  • 两宫候问，宫使交至 - Narrative expression meaning 'messengers from both palaces came to inquire, and palace attendants arrived in succession.'

  • 或以人名香，故有朝姝香、丽居香、洛珍香、洁华香 - Means 'sometimes the scents were named after the women, hence there were 'Morning Beauty Fragrance,' 'Beautiful Dwelling Fragrance,' 'Luozhen Fragrance,' and 'Pure Splendor Fragrance,' naming the individual scents.
  • 亮每游，此四人皆同舆席 - Means 'Whenever Sun Liang traveled, these four women would accompany him in the same carriage,' indicating their constant presence.
  • 来侍皆以香名，前后为次，不得越乱 - Means 'The attendants were named after their fragrances, and they followed a strict order, never to be disordered,' describing the hierarchy and rules.
  • 所居之室，名为 “思香媚寝” - Means 'The room where they stayed was called 'Thoughtful Fragrant Retreat,' naming the room.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 命宫人 - 命 means 'order' or 'command,' and 宫人 refers to a palace attendant or concubine. Together, it means 'ordered a palace woman.'
  • 作玻璃屏凤 - 作 means 'to make' or 'to create,' 玻璃 refers to glass, 屏 means 'screen' or 'partition,' and 凤 means 'phoenix.' This phrase describes the creation of a glass screen in the shape of a phoenix.
  • 镂祥物一百三十种 - 镂 means 'to carve' or 'to engrave,' 祥物 refers to auspicious objects, and 一百三十种 means 'one hundred and thirty kinds.' This phrase describes the carving of one hundred and thirty auspicious objects.
  • 各有生气 - 各有 means 'each has,' and 生气 means 'vitality' or 'life force.' This phrase indicates that each object has a lifelike appearance.
  • 远视若真 - 远视 means 'when viewed from a distance,' and 若真 means 'as if real.' This phrase describes how the objects appear lifelike when seen from afar.
  • 触屏，坠其一凤 - 触屏 means 'touched the screen,' and 坠其一凤 means 'one phoenix fell.' This describes an incident where one phoenix fell from the screen.
  • 顿之飞去 - 顿之 means 'to strike it,' and 飞去 means 'flew away.' This phrase describes the phoenix flying away after being struck.
  • 生于微贱 - 生于 means 'born in,' and 微贱 means 'lowly' or 'humble.' This phrase indicates that the woman was born in humble circumstances.
  • 里中相者云 - 里中 means 'in the village,' 相者 means 'fortune-teller' or 'diviner,' and 云 means 'said.' This phrase introduces a fortune-teller's prediction.
  • 位极宫掖 - 位极 means 'reached the highest position,' and 宫掖 refers to the inner palace. This phrase predicts that the woman would reach the highest position in the palace.
  • 玉质柔肌 - 玉质 means 'jade-like quality,' and 柔肌 means 'soft skin.' This phrase describes the woman's physical beauty.
  • 态媚容冶 - 态媚 means 'charming demeanor,' and 容冶 means 'beautiful appearance.' This phrase describes the woman's attractive appearance.
  • 致白绡帐中 - 致 means 'to bring,' 白绡 means 'white gauze,' and 帐中 means 'in the tent.' This phrase describes the woman being brought into a white gauze tent.
  • 于户外望者，如月下聚雪 - 于户外望者 means 'those who looked from outside,' 如 means 'like,' 月下 means 'under the moon,' and 聚雪 means 'gathered snow.' This phrase describes how the woman appeared from outside the tent, like gathered snow under the moon.
  • 河南献玉人 - 河南 means 'from Henan,' 献 means 'to present,' and 玉人 means 'jade figure.' This phrase describes a jade figure presented from Henan.
  • 高三尺 - 高 means 'tall,' and 三尺 means 'three feet.' This phrase describes the height of the jade figure.
  • 乃取玉人置后侧 - 乃 means 'then,' 取 means 'to take,' 置 means 'to place,' and 后侧 means 'next to the woman.' This phrase describes the jade figure being placed next to the woman.
  • 昼则讲说军谋 - 昼 means 'during the day,' 则 means 'then,' 讲说 means 'to discuss,' and 军谋 means 'military strategy.' This phrase describes the woman discussing military strategy during the day.
  • 夕则拥后而玩玉人 - 夕 means 'at night,' 拥 means 'to embrace,' 玩 means 'to play with,' and 玉人 means 'the jade figure.' This phrase describes the man embracing the woman and playing with the jade figure at night.
  • 玉之所贵，比德君子 - 玉之所贵 means 'the value of jade,' 比德 means 'to compare virtues,' and 君子 means 'gentleman.' This phrase compares the virtues of jade to those of a gentleman.
  • 况为人形，而可不玩乎 - 况 means 'how much more,' 为人形 means 'in human form,' 而 means 'and,' and 可不玩乎 means 'how can one not play with it?' This phrase questions why one would not play with a jade figure in human form.
  • 甘后与玉人洁白齐润 - 甘后 refers to the woman, 玉人 refers to the jade figure, 洁白 means 'white and clean,' and 齐润 means 'equally smooth.' This phrase describes how the woman and the jade figure were equally white and smooth.
  • 观者殆相惑乱 - 观者 means 'those who observe,' 殆 means 'almost,' and 相惑乱 means 'confused each other.' This phrase describes how observers were almost confused between the woman and the jade figure.
  • 嬖宠者非惟嫉于甘后，亦妒于玉人也 - 嬖宠者 means 'those who are favored,' 非惟 means 'not only,' 嫉 means 'envy,' 于 means 'towards,' 亦 means 'also,' and 妒 means 'jealousy.' This phrase describes how those who were favored not only envied the woman but also the jade figure.
  • 后常欲取玉人琢毁之 - 后 refers to the woman, 常 means 'often,' 欲 means 'to want,' 取 means 'to take,' 琢毁 means 'to carve and destroy,' and 之 means 'it.' This phrase describes the woman's desire to destroy the jade figure.
  • 乃诫先主曰 - 乃 means 'then,' 诫 means 'to warn,' 先主 refers to the emperor, and 曰 means 'said.' This phrase describes the woman warning the emperor.
  • 昔子罕不以玉为宝，春秋美之 - 昔 means 'in the past,' 子罕 refers to a historical figure, 不以 means 'did not consider,' 玉为宝 means 'jade as a treasure,' 春秋 refers to the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, and 美之 means 'praised it.' This phrase refers to a historical event where a person did not consider jade as a treasure, and it was praised in the *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
  • 今吴魏未灭，安以妖玩经怀 - 今 means 'now,' 吴魏 refers to Wu and Wei, 未灭 means 'not yet defeated,' 安 means 'how can,' 以 means 'with,' 妖玩 means '妖孽玩物' (妖孽: evil spirits, 玩物: playthings), and 经怀 means 'occupy one's mind.' This phrase questions how one can occupy their mind with evil playthings when Wu and Wei have not yet been defeated.
  • 凡淫惑生疑，勿复进焉 - 凡 means 'all,' 淫惑 means 'deception and temptation,' 生疑 means 'to cause doubt,' 勿 means 'do not,' 复 means 'again,' 进 means 'to advance,' and 焉 is a particle. This phrase advises against advancing in matters of deception and temptation.
  • 先主乃撤玉人，嬖者皆退 - 先主 refers to the emperor, 乃 means 'then,' 撤 means 'to remove,' 玉人 refers to the jade figure, 嬖者 means 'those who are favored,' and 皆退 means 'all withdrew.' This phrase describes the emperor removing the jade figure and those who were favored withdrawing.
  • 当时君子，议以甘后为神智夫人焉 - 当时 means 'at that time,' 君子 means 'gentlemen,' 议 means 'to discuss,' 以 means 'to consider,' 甘后 refers to the woman, 为 means 'as,' 神智夫人 means 'divine and wise lady,' and 焉 is a particle. This phrase describes how the gentlemen at that time considered the woman a divine and wise lady.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 齊廢帝東昏侯 - Emperor Donghun of Qi, who ruled from 494 to 498 AD, was known for his excessive luxury and indulgence.
  • 潘妃 - Empress Pan, the wife of Emperor Donghun, was famous for her beauty and the influence she wielded over the emperor.
  • 神仙、永壽二殿 - These were two grand halls built by Emperor Donghun, symbolizing his desire for immortality and longevity.
  • 玉壽中作飛仙帳 - The flying fairy canopy was a symbol of the emperor's wish to ascend to heaven.
  • 四回繡綺 - The intricate embroidery on the curtains reflects the high level of craftsmanship and luxury in the palace.
  • 靈獸神禽，風雲華炬 - These decorations were common in imperial architecture, representing power, prosperity, and good fortune.
  • 椽桷之端悉垂鈴佩 - The use of bells and pendants on the beams and rafters created a pleasant sound, adding to the palace's ambiance.
  • 江左舊物 - The Jiangnan region was known for its rich cultural heritage, and the artifacts from this area were highly valued.
  • 古玉律數枚 - Jade tubes were used in ancient Chinese musical instruments, and their repurposing for the emperor's flute shows the emperor's interest in music.
  • 莊嚴寺有玉九子鈴 - The Nine Sons Bell was a significant artifact, often used in religious and ceremonial contexts.
  • 外國寺佛面有光相 - The light-emitting features of the Buddha statues were a symbol of spiritual enlightenment.
  • 禪靈寺塔諸寶珥 - The Chanling Temple was a prominent religious site, and its tower was adorned with precious ornaments.
  • 皆剝取以施潘妃殿飾 - The stripping of decorations from temples and other sacred places for the emperor's palace highlights the emperor's disregard for religious and cultural values.
  • 又鑿為蓮花以貼地 - The lotus pattern is a common symbol in Chinese culture, representing purity and beauty.
  • 令潘妃行其上，曰：“此步步生蓮花也。” - This phrase is a metaphor for the beauty and grace of Empress Pan.
  • 涂壁皆以麝香 - Musk was a highly prized fragrance, and its use in the palace walls indicates the emperor's wealth and luxury.
  • 錦幔珠簾，窮極綺麗 - The luxurious curtains and pearl curtains reflect the opulence of the palace.
  • 執役工匠，自夜達曉 - The continuous labor of the workers shows the intensity of the construction and decoration efforts.
  • 猶不速副 - The inability to complete the task quickly enough highlights the scale and complexity of the projects.
  • 乃剔取諸佛寺刹殿藻 - The stripping of decorations from temples and other sacred places for the emperor's palace shows the emperor's disregard for religious and cultural values.
  • 並仙人騎獸以充之 - The inclusion of depictions of immortals riding mythical creatures adds to the fantastical and luxurious nature of the palace.
  • 武帝興光樓上施青漆 - Emperor Wu of the Southern Song dynasty was known for his architectural projects, and the use of green lacquer on the Xingguang Tower was a notable feature.
  • 世人謂之“青樓” - The 'Green Tower' was a famous landmark in the capital city.
  • 帝曰：“武帝不巧，何不純用琉璃！” - The emperor's criticism of Emperor Wu's use of green lacquer shows his preference for more luxurious materials like glass.
  • 潘氏服御，極選珍寶 - Empress Pan's clothing and accessories were made from the finest and most precious materials, reflecting her status and the emperor's wealth.
  • 主衣庫舊物不復用 - The disposal of old items from the imperial wardrobe indicates the emperor's constant pursuit of new and more luxurious items.
  • 用貴市人間金银寶物，價皆數倍 - The high prices of the jewelry and precious objects purchased from the market show the emperor's willingness to spend vast amounts of money on luxury items.
  • 琥珀钗一只，值百七十万 - The high value of the amber hairpin demonstrates the extreme wealth and luxury of the palace.
  • 潘妃生女，百日而亡 - The death of Empress Pan's daughter after 100 days was a tragic event that deeply affected the royal family.
  • 制斬衰杖衣，悉粗布 - The use of coarse cloth for mourning attire shows the traditional and somber nature of the mourning period.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 群小来吊，盘旋地坐，举手受执蔬膳，积旬不听音伎 — This sequence describes the behavior of the officials, which must be read as a continuous series of actions.
  • 左右直长阉竖王宝孙诸人，共营肴羞，云为天子解菜 — This clause introduces a group of influential figures and their actions, which must be read as a collective effort.
  • 于苑中立店肆，模大市，日游市中，杂所货物，与人、阉竖共为裨贩 — This sequence describes the establishment and operation of commercial activities, which must be read as a series of related actions.
  • 以潘妃为市令，自为市吏，录事将斗者就潘妃罚之 — This clause introduces the roles and responsibilities of Pan Fei and the emperor, which must be read as a system of governance.
  • 帝小有得失，潘则与杖，乃敕虎贲威仪，不得进大荆子，阁内不得进实中获 — This sequence describes the emperor's strict control and the consequences of his actions, which must be read as a series of commands and reactions.
  • 虽畏潘氏，而窃与诸姊妹淫通 — This clause introduces a hidden and forbidden relationship, which must be read as a series of actions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 又有女巫子杨珉之 - The term '女巫子' (daughter of a female witch) could be ambiguous. It is more likely that '杨珉之' is the name of the female witch.
  • 与同寝处，如伉俪 - The term '如伉俪' (as if they were a married couple) is a metaphorical expression that needs to be clearly conveyed in English.
  • 及太孙即帝位，为皇后 - The term '即帝位' (ascended to the throne) is a formal way of describing the coronation.
  • 封后嫡母刘为高昌县都乡君 - The term '封后嫡母' (enfeoffed the empress's legitimate mother) is a formal description of the enfeoffment.
  • 所生母宋为徐杭广昌乡君 - The term '所生母' (biological mother) is a specific term that needs to be clearly defined.
  • 镜在床元因堕地 - The term '因堕地' (fell to the ground) is a straightforward description but needs to be placed in the correct context.
  • 与太后同日谒太庙 - The term '谒太庙' (paid homage to the Imperial Ancestral Temple) is a formal ritual.
  • 杨珉之为帝所幸，常居中侍 - The term '常居中侍' (often served in the inner palace) is a formal description of her role.
  • 明帝为辅，与王宴、徐孝嗣、王广之并面请，不听 - The term '面请' (personally requested) emphasizes the directness of the request.
  • 皇后与帝同席坐，流涕覆面 - The term '流涕覆面' (covering her face with tears) describes her emotional state.
  • 杨郎好少年，无罪过，何可狂杀 - The term '何可狂杀' (why should he be killed so recklessly) is a rhetorical question.
  • 此事别有一意，不可令人闻 - The term '别有一意' (has a different intention) suggests a hidden agenda.
  • 阿奴暂去 - The term '阿奴' (my dear) is an affectionate term used by the emperor.
  • 外间并云，杨氓之与皇后有异情，彰闻遐迩 - The term '异情' (improper relationship) is a euphemism for adultery.
  • 但之驰报明帝，即令建康行刑，而果有敕原之，而氓之已死 - The term '驰报' (immediately reported) emphasizes the urgency.
  • 后既淫乱，又与帝相爱亵，故帝恣之 - The terms '淫乱' (promiscuous) and '相爱亵' (intimate) describe her behavior.
  • 又迎后亲戚人宫，常赐人百数十万 - The term '人宫' (into the palace) and '常赐人百数十万' (often granting them hundreds of thousands of coins) describe her generosity.
  • 以武帝曜灵殿处后家属 - The term '曜灵殿' (Yao Ling Hall) is a specific location.
  • 帝废，后为皇妃 - The term '皇妃' (royal concubine) is a lower rank than empress.
  • 父戢自有传 - The term '自有传' (has his own biography) indicates that there is a separate account of him.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初 - Indicates the beginning of an event or period.
  • 有折冲将军元正列于邺城东水中得玺以献 - A complex phrase describing a military officer named Yuan Zheng who found a seal in the waters east of Yecheng and presented it.
  • 文曰“天皇后玺” - The inscription on the seal reads 'Seal of the Heavenly Empress.'
  • 盖石氏所作 - Indicates that the seal was made by the Shi family.
  • 诏书颁告，以为穆后之瑞焉 - The imperial edict announced it as a sign of good fortune for Empress Mu.
  • 武成时，为胡后造真珠裙裤 - During the reign of Wu Cheng, a pearl skirt and trousers were made for Empress Hu.
  • 所费不可胜计 - The cost was immeasurable.
  • 被火所烧 - It was burned by fire.
  • 后主既立，穆皇后复为营之 - After the new emperor ascended the throne, Empress Mu had another one made.
  • 属周武，遭太后丧 - During the time of Emperor Wu of Zhou, she encountered the death of the empress dowager.
  • 诏侍中薛孤、康买等为吊使 - The emperor ordered Zhongshi Xuegu and Kang Mai to serve as envoys of condolence.
  • 又遣商胡赍锦三万匹，与吊使同往 - He also sent a merchant to carry 30,000 bolts of silk to accompany the envoys.
  • 欲市真珠为皇后造七宝车 - To buy pearls to make a seven-jeweled carriage for the empress.
  • 周人不与交易 - The Zhou people refused to trade.
  • 然而竟造焉 - Nevertheless, it was eventually made.
  • 先是，童谣曰“黄花势欲落，清觞满杯酌。” - Before this, a children's rhyme said, 'The yellow flower is about to fall, the clear wine fills the cup.'
  • 言黄花不久也 - This means that the yellow flower would not last long.
  • 后主自立穆后以后昏饮无度 - The new emperor, after establishing Empress Mu, drank excessively.
  • 故云“清觞满杯酌。” - Therefore, it says 'the clear wine fills the cup.'
  • 陆怠骆提婆诏改姓为穆 - By imperial decree, Lu Tai and Luo Tiduo were ordered to change their surnames to Mu.
  • 陆封大姬，皆以皇后故也 - Lu Feng and Da Ji, both because of Empress Mu.
  • 后既以陆为母，提婆为家，更不目轻霄 - Afterward, Empress Mu considered Lu as her mother and Tiduo as her family, and she no longer looked down on Qingxiao.
  • 轻霄自辽西欲求见太后、陆媪，使禁掌之，竟不得见 - Qingxiao from Liaoxi wanted to see the empress dowager and Lady Lu, but they were forbidden and he could not see them.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 舊相傳 - A phrase indicating that something is passed down through generations or widely believed.
  • 有聖人跡 - Literally means 'traces of a sage,' referring to something believed to be a sign of divine or sage presence.
  • 淑妃欲往觀之 - '淑妃' refers to a virtuous concubine or secondary wife, and '欲往觀之' means she wanted to go and see it.
  • 帝恐弩矢及橋 - '帝' refers to the emperor, and '恐弩矢及橋' means he was afraid that arrows would hit the bridge.
  • 抽攻城木造遠橋 - '抽攻城木' means to take down siege equipment, and '造遠橋' means to build a long bridge.
  • 監作舍人，以不造成受罰 - '監作舍人' refers to the supervisor of the construction, and '以不造成受罰' means he was punished for not completing the bridge.
  • 帝與淑妃渡橋，橋壞 - '帝與淑妃渡橋' means the emperor and the concubine crossed the bridge, and '橋壞' means the bridge collapsed.
  • 稱妃有功勳，將立為左皇后 - '稱妃有功勳' means the concubine was praised for her merit, and '將立為左皇后' means she was about to be made the left empress.
  • 即令使驰取翟等皇后服御 - '即令使驰取' means immediately ordered someone to rush and fetch, and '翟等皇后服御' refers to the ceremonial robes of a queen.
  • 仍與之并骑观战 - '仍與之并骑' means he still rode with her, and '观战' means to watch the battle.
  • 东偏少却 - '东偏' refers to the eastern side, and '少却' means it slightly retreated.
  • 淑妃怖曰：“军败矣。” - '淑妃怖' means the concubine was terrified, and '军败矣' means the army has been defeated.
  • 帝遂以淑妃奔 - '帝遂以淑妃奔' means the emperor then fled with the concubine.
  • 还至洪洞戍 - '还至洪洞戍' means they returned to the garrison at Hongtong.
  • 淑妃以粉镜自玩 - '淑妃以粉镜自玩' means the concubine was playing with a powder mirror.
  • 后声乱唱贼至 - '后声乱唱贼至' means someone later shouted that the enemy had arrived.
  • 内参自晋阳以皇后衣至 - '内参' refers to an inner official, and '自晋阳以皇后衣至' means he came from Jinyang with the empress's clothing.
  • 帝为按辔，命淑妃着之，然后去 - '帝为按辔' means the emperor controlled his horse, and '命淑妃着之，然后去' means he ordered the concubine to put on the clothes and then left.
  • 帝奔邺，太后后至，帝不出迎 - '帝奔邺' means the emperor fled to Yecheng, and '太后后至，帝不出迎' means the empress dowager arrived later, but the emperor did not come out to greet her.
  • 淑妃将至，凿城北门，出十里迎之 - '淑妃将至' means the concubine was about to arrive, and '凿城北门，出十里迎之' means he dug a northern gate in the city and went ten li to meet her.
  • 复以淑妃奔青州 - '复以淑妃奔青州' means he again fled with the concubine to Qingzhou.
  • 后主至长安，问周武帝乞淑妃 - '后主' refers to the emperor, and '问周武帝乞淑妃' means he asked Emperor Wu of Zhou for the concubine.
  • 帝曰：“朕视天下如脱，一老妪岂与公惜也。” - '朕视天下如脱' means the emperor saw the world as something he could easily discard, and '一老妪岂与公惜也' means an old woman is not worth sparing.
  • 仍以赐之 - '仍以赐之' means he still gave her to him.
  • 及帝遇害，以淑妃赐代王达，甚嬖之 - '及帝遇害' means when the emperor was killed, and '以淑妃赐代王达，甚嬖之' means he gave the concubine to Prince Dai and greatly favored her.
  • 淑妃弹琵琶，因弦断作诗曰 - '淑妃弹琵琶' means the concubine played the pipa, and '因弦断作诗曰' means she wrote a poem when the string broke.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 后主张贵妃 - Posthumously enfeoffed as the Noble Consort Zhang; likely refers to Empress Zhang Lihua (張麗華), a historical figure during the Southern Dynasties.
  • 兵家女 - A daughter of a military family.
  • 以选入宫 - Chosen to enter the palace; suggests a formal selection process, possibly through a concubine selection.
  • 侍龚贵嫔为良娣 - Served as a concubine to Princess Gong; indicates her initial position in the palace.
  • 后主见而悦之 - The emperor saw her and was pleased; describes the emperor's reaction upon seeing her.
  • 因得幸 - And thus gained favor; indicates she became favored by the emperor.
  • 遂有娠 - And became pregnant; indicates she became pregnant as a result of her favor.
  • 生太子深 - Gave birth to Crown Prince Shen; specifies the child she bore.
  • 拜为贵妃 - Was enfeoffed as a Noble Consort; indicates her elevated status.
  • 性聪慧 - She was intelligent and wise; describes her character.
  • 甚被宠遇 - She was greatly favored; emphasizes her high status.
  • 后主始以始兴王叔陵之乱被伤 - The emperor was initially injured by the rebellion of Prince Shixing; provides context for the emperor's condition.
  • 卧于承香殿 - Lay in the Xiangcheng Hall; indicates the location where the emperor was resting.
  • 时诸姬并不得进 - At that time, all the consorts were not allowed to enter; describes the situation in the palace.
  • 惟贵妃侍焉 - Only the Noble Consort attended to him; highlights her unique position.
  • 而柳太后犹居柏梁殿 - While Empress Dowager Liu still resided in the Bailiang Hall; provides additional context about the imperial family.
  • 即皇后之正殿也 - Which was the main hall of the empress; clarifies the significance of the hall.
  • 而阮皇后素无宠于后主 - But Empress Ruan had never been favored by the emperor; explains her lack of influence.
  • 不得侍疾 - Could not attend to his illness; indicates her exclusion from the emperor's care.
  • 别居求贤殿 - Resided separately in the Qiuxian Hall; describes her living situation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 饰以金玉，间以珠翠 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'adorned with gold and jade, interspersed with pearls and gemstones.'
  • 外施珠帘，内有宝床宝帐 - Means 'outside adorned with pearl curtains, inside with precious beds and canopies.'
  • 服玩之属 - Refers to 'ornaments and playthings.'
  • 瑰奇珍丽 - Means 'rare, strange, precious, and beautiful.'
  • 复道交相往来 - Means 'a double path allowing mutual passage.'
  • 王李二美人、张薛二淑媛、袁昭仪、何捷妤、江修容等七人 - Refers to 'seven women, including Wang and Li two beauties, Zhang and Xue two gentlewomen, Yuan the Attendant, He the Swift Lady, and Jiang the Beautiful Lady.'
  • 递代以游其上 - Means 'they took turns to enjoy the upper levels.'
  • 以宫人有文学者袁大舍等为女学士 - Means 'the emperor appointed palace women who were literate, such as Yuan Dasha, as female scholars.'
  • 每引宾客对贵妃等游宴 - Means 'every time he invited guests to enjoy banquets with the Concubine Zhang and others.'
  • 使诸贵人及女学士与狎客共赋新诗，互相赠答 - Means 'he had the concubines, female scholars, and close companions compose new poems together, which they exchanged with each other.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 后主 - Refers to the last emperor of the Southern Qi Dynasty, Xiao Yan, who was known for his literary pursuits and luxurious lifestyle.
  • 张贵妃 - Refers to Zhang Huaibian, one of the most famous concubines of Xiao Yan.
  • 龚、孔二贵嫔 - Refers to Gong and Kong, two other concubines of high rank.
  • 王李二美人、张薛二淑媛、袁昭仪、何捷妤、江修容 - Refers to other concubines and ladies-in-waiting in the imperial harem.
  • 女学士 - Refers to women who were educated and appointed to positions similar to those of male scholars.
  • 狎客 - Refers to close companions or favorites who were often involved in literary and artistic activities.
  • 积石为山，引水为池 - Describes the artificial landscape created within the palace, with rocks piled up to form mountains and water channels to create ponds.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 湖上柳 - Literal phrase meaning 'willows by the lake,' used to describe the setting of the poem.
  • 烟里不胜垂 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the willows droop gracefully in the mist,' emphasizing the willows' delicate appearance.
  • 宿露洗开明媚眼 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the morning dew washes open the bright eyes,' suggesting the willows' fresh and vibrant appearance after the night.
  • 东风摇弄好腰枝 - Poetic phrase meaning 'the east wind gently sways the slender branches,' highlighting the willows' graceful movement.
  • 烟雨更相宜 - Phrase meaning 'the mist and rain make the scene even more beautiful,' emphasizing the harmonious atmosphere.
  • 环曲岸 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'circling the curved shore,' describing the willows' position around the lake.
  • 阴覆画桥低 - Phrase meaning 'casting shadows over the low painted bridge,' suggesting the willows' protective and decorative role.
  • 线拂行人春晚后 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the willow branches lightly touch passersby after the late spring,' emphasizing the willows' gentle interaction with people.
  • 絮飞晴雪暖风时 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the willow fluff flies like snow in the warm breeze,' evoking a vivid image of the willows' seeds dispersing.
  • 幽意更依依 - Phrase meaning 'the willows exude a deeper, lingering charm,' conveying the willows' subtle and enduring beauty.
  • 湖上雪 - Literal phrase meaning 'snow by the lake,' setting the scene for the second part of the poem.
  • 风急堕还多 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the wind blows, and the snowflakes fall in abundance,' emphasizing the dynamic and abundant nature of the snowfall.
  • 轻片有时敲竹户 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'light snowflakes sometimes tap on bamboo doors,' creating a sense of quiet and intimacy.
  • 素华无韵入澄波 - Poetic phrase meaning 'pure white flowers without melody enter the clear water,' suggesting the snow's purity and its gentle interaction with the water.
  • 望外玉相磨 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'from afar, the snowflakes seem to rub against each other like jade,' emphasizing the snow's smooth and delicate appearance.
  • 湖水远，天地色相和 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the lake water stretches far, blending with the colors of the sky and earth,' evoking a sense of vastness and harmony.
  • 仰面莫思梁苑赋 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'look up and do not think of the *Liang Yuan Fu* (赋),' suggesting a moment of reflection and detachment from worldly concerns.
  • 朝来且听玉人歌 - Phrase meaning 'in the morning, listen to the song of the jade maiden,' evoking a sense of beauty and serenity.
  • 不醉拟如何 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how can one not be intoxicated by such beauty,' expressing the poet's admiration and emotional response.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 梁苑赋 - Refers to the *Liang Yuan Fu* (赋), a famous literary work by Jia Yi, a Han dynasty writer known for his essays and poetry. This allusion suggests a moment of reflection on the beauty of nature and the transient nature of human endeavors.
  • 玉人歌 - A metaphorical reference to a beautiful and enchanting song, often associated with the idealized beauty of women in classical Chinese literature.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 湖上柳，烟里不胜垂，宿露洗开明媚眼，东风摇弄好腰枝。烟雨更相宜。This sequence describes the willows in a series of vivid and interconnected images, each building upon the previous one to create a cohesive and evocative scene.
  • 环曲岸，阴覆画桥低，线拂行人春晚后，絮飞晴雪暖风时。幽意更依依。This sequence continues the description of the willows, focusing on their interaction with the environment and the people around them.
  • 湖上雪，风急堕还多。轻片有时敲竹户，素华无韵入澄波。望外玉相磨。This sequence shifts to the winter scene, describing the snowfall and its effects on the lake and surrounding area.
  • 湖水远，天地色相和。仰面莫思梁苑赋，朝来且听玉人歌。不醉拟如何？This sequence concludes the poem with a reflective and serene tone, suggesting a moment of peace and beauty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 西苑风景台殿如此，陛下岂不思恋，舍之而远游也 - Means 'The scenery and buildings of the Western Garden are so beautiful, how could Your Majesty not miss them and leave them to travel far away?' expressing nostalgia and a plea to stay.
  • 左右甚疑讶 - Means 'those around him were very suspicious and surprised,' indicating the reaction of the emperor's attendants.
  • 帝御龙舟，中道，夜半，闻歌者甚悲 - Means 'The emperor was on the dragon boat, in the middle of the journey, at midnight, and heard a very sad song,' setting the scene for the song.
  • 我兄征辽东，饿死青山下 - Means 'My brother went to fight in Liaodong and starved to death under the green hills,' a line from the song.
  • 令我挽龙舟，又困隋堤道 - Means 'He made me pull the dragon boat, and I was also trapped on the Sui Canal,' another line from the song.
  • 方今天下饥，路粮无些少 - Means 'Nowadays, the whole country is hungry, and there is no food on the road,' a line from the song.
  • 前去三十程，此身安可保 - Means 'Going ahead thirty stages, how can my body be safe?' a line from the song.
  • 寒骨枕荒沙，幽魂泣烟草 - Means 'My cold bones lie on the barren sand, and my lonely spirit weeps among the withered grass,' a line from the song.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 悲損閨內妻 - Expresses sorrow for a spouse; rendered as 'Grieving for the wife within the inner chamber.'
  • 望斷吾家老 - Indicates deep longing and absence; rendered as 'Looking in vain for my aged family.'
  • 安得義男兒 - Expresses a wish for a virtuous individual; rendered as 'How to obtain a righteous man.'
  • 引其孤魂回 - Refers to guiding a spirit back from the dead; rendered as 'To bring back the lonely spirit.'
  • 負其白骨歸 - Indicates the act of burying remains; rendered as 'To carry its white bones home.'
  • 帝聞其歌 - Refers to the emperor listening to a song; rendered as 'The emperor heard the song.'
  • 遂遣人求其歌者 - Indicates the emperor's response to the song; rendered as 'Then sent people to seek the singer.'
  • 至曉不得其人 - Indicates the search's outcome; rendered as 'Until dawn, the person was not found.'
  • 帝頗彷徨 - Expresses the emperor's emotional state; rendered as 'The emperor was greatly distressed.'
  • 通夕不寐 - Indicates the emperor's insomnia; rendered as 'All night without sleep.'
  • 無一人至者 - Indicates the absence of expected visitors; rendered as 'Not a single person arrived.'
  • 有來者，在途，遭兵奪其貢物 - Describes the fate of travelers; rendered as 'Those who came were on the road, and their tribute was seized by soldiers.'
  • 帝猶與群臣議 - Indicates ongoing deliberation; rendered as 'The emperor still discussed with his ministers.'
  • 詔十三道起兵 - Indicates a military command; rendered as 'The emperor ordered thirteen regions to raise armies.'
  • 天下朝貢使 - Refers to ambassadors or envoys who bring tribute to the emperor; rendered as 'Ambassadors from all over the country.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 閨內妻 - Refers to the wife within the inner chamber, a common term for a wife in classical Chinese.
  • 義男兒 - Refers to a righteous man, a virtuous individual.
  • 孤魂 - Refers to a lonely spirit, often associated with the dead.
  • 白骨 - Refers to the bones of the deceased, often used in funerary contexts.
  • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, the ruler of the empire.
  • 十三道 - Refers to the thirteen administrative regions of the empire.
  • 朝貢使 - Refers to ambassadors or envoys who bring tribute to the emperor.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 悲損閨內妻，望斷吾家老 - This couplet expresses the emperor's sorrow for his wife and longing for his family.
  • 帝聞其歌，遂遣人求其歌者，至曉不得其人 - This sequence describes the emperor's reaction to the song and the outcome of the search.
  • 帝頗彷徨，通夕不寐 - This couplet describes the emperor's emotional state and insomnia.
  • 無一人至者 - This phrase indicates the absence of expected visitors.
  • 有來者，在途，遭兵奪其貢物 - This clause describes the fate of travelers.
  • 帝猶與群臣議，詔十三道起兵 - This sequence describes the emperor's ongoing deliberation and military command.
  • 天下朝貢使，無一人至者 - This couplet indicates the absence of ambassadors.

Proper Nouns

  • 帝 - The emperor, the ruler of the empire.
  • 十三道 - The thirteen administrative regions of the empire.
  • 朝貢使 - Ambassadors or envoys who bring tribute to the emperor.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 翌日 - Literally 'the next day,' used to mark a sequence of events.
  • 臣本出南楚卑薄之地 - A humble self-description meaning 'I originally came from a humble place in the southern region of Chu.'
  • 逢聖明為治之时 - Means 'I encountered a time of wise and enlightened governance.'
  • 不爱此身，愿从入贡 - A formal expression of loyalty, meaning 'I do not value my own life, but I wish to serve and contribute.'
  • 臣本侏儒，性尤蒙滞 - A self-deprecating statement, meaning 'I am originally a short person, and my nature is particularly dull and slow.'
  • 出入左右，积有岁华 - Means 'I have been in and out of your presence for many years.'
  • 浓被圣私，皆逾素望 - Means 'I have received great favor from Your Majesty, surpassing my original expectations.'
  • 侍从乘舆，周旋台阁 - Means 'I have served as an attendant to Your Majesty's carriage and have been active in the imperial court.'
  • 酷好穷经 - Means 'I have a strong passion for studying the classics.'
  • 颇知善恶之本源 - Means 'I have a good understanding of the root causes of good and evil.'
  • 少识兴亡之所以 - Means 'I have some knowledge of the reasons for rise and fall.'
  • 还往民间，周知利害 - Means 'I have traveled among the common people and understand the advantages and disadvantages.'
  • 深蒙顾问，方敢敷陈 - Means 'I have been deeply consulted, and only then do I dare to present my views.'
  • 自陛下嗣守元符，体临大器 - Means 'Since Your Majesty ascended the throne and assumed the great responsibility.'
  • 圣神独断，谏净莫从 - Means 'Your Majesty has made decisions alone, and no one can offer advice.'
  • 独发睿谋，不容人献 - Means 'Your Majesty has made wise decisions without allowing others to contribute.'
  • 大兴西苑，两至辽东 - Means 'You have greatly expanded the Western Garden and twice traveled to Liaodong.'
  • 龙舟逾于万艘 - Means 'The dragon boats number more than ten thousand.'
  • 宫阙遍乎天下 - Means 'Palaces and halls are spread throughout the land.'
  • 兵甲常役百万 - Means 'Military forces are always in the hundreds of thousands.'
  • 士民穷乎山谷 - Means 'The people are impoverished in the mountains and valleys.'
  • 征辽者百不存十 - Means 'Those who went to conquer Liaodong, only one in ten survived.'
  • 殁葬者十未有一 - Means 'Those who died, only one in ten was buried.'
  • 帑藏全虚，谷粟涌贵 - Means 'The treasury is completely empty, and grain prices have skyrocketed.'
  • 乘舆竟往，行幸无时 - Means 'Your Majesty has traveled extensively, with no fixed schedule.'
  • 兵士时从，常逾万人 - Means 'The soldiers follow, often numbering more than ten thousand.'
  • 遂令四方失望，天下为墟 - Means 'This has led to disappointment throughout the land, and the world has become desolate.'
  • 方今有家之村，存者可数 - Means 'Now, villages that once had homes are few and far between.'
  • 子弟死于兵役，老弱困于蓬蒿 - Means 'The young men have died in military service, and the elderly and weak are trapped in the wilderness.'
  • 兵尸如岳，饿殍盈郊 - Means 'The dead bodies of soldiers are like mountains, and the starving corpses fill the outskirts.'
  • 狗彘厌人之肉，乌食人之余 - Means 'Dogs and pigs are fed with human flesh, and birds eat the leftovers.'
  • 臭闻千里，骨积高原 - Means 'The stench is smelled for miles, and bones pile up on highlands.'
  • 膏血草野，狐犬尽肥 - Means 'Blood and flesh are scattered in the wild, and foxes and dogs have become fat.'
  • 阴风元人之墟，鬼哭寒草之下 - Means 'The wind howls over the ruins of human settlements, and ghosts cry under the cold grass.'
  • 万民剥落，不保朝昏 - Means 'The common people are suffering, and they cannot even ensure their survival from morning to night.'
  • 父遗幼子，妻号故夫 - Means 'Fathers leave behind their young sons, and wives mourn their deceased husbands.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 元符 - Refers to the era name of Emperor Zhenzong of Song, who reigned from 997 to 1022.
  • 西苑 - Refers to the Western Garden, a famous imperial garden in the capital city.
  • 辽东 - Refers to Liaodong, a region in northeastern China.

  • 乌 - Refers to birds, often used to symbolize scavengers in classical Chinese literature.
  • 阴风 - Refers to cold, eerie winds, often associated with desolation and death.
  • 鬼哭 - Refers to the wailing of ghosts, a common motif in classical Chinese literature to depict desolation and suffering.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 孤苦何多 - Rhetorical question meaning 'How many are left in solitary misery?'
  • 饿殍尤甚 - Means 'starvation and death are even more severe,' emphasizing the dire situation.
  • 乱离方始 - Means 'the chaos and exile are just beginning,' indicating the onset of turmoil.
  • 生死孰知 - Rhetorical question meaning 'who can know life and death?' expressing the uncertainty of fate.
  • 人主爱人，一何如此 - Rhetorical question meaning 'How does the ruler love his people so much?' questioning the ruler's benevolence.
  • 陛下恒性毅然 - Means 'Your Majesty's constant nature is resolute,' describing the emperor's steadfast character.
  • 孰敢上谏 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Who dares to remonstrate?' expressing the fear of speaking out.
  • 或有鲠言，又令赐死 - Means 'If there is anyone who speaks frankly, they are ordered to be executed,' describing the emperor's harsh response to criticism.
  • 臣下相顾，钳结自全 - Means 'Ministers look at each other, clench their jaws, and seek to preserve themselves,' describing the ministers' fear and self-preservation.
  • 龙逢复生，安敢议奏 - Means 'If even Dragon-Feng were to be reborn, how dare they advise?' referring to the legendary advisor who was executed for speaking truth to power.
  • 左右近臣，阿谀顺旨 - Means 'Those around the emperor, flatter and follow his wishes,' describing the courtiers' sycophantic behavior.
  • 迎合帝意，造作拒谏 - Means 'They cater to the emperor's wishes and fabricate excuses to reject remonstrance,' describing the courtiers' manipulative tactics.
  • 皆出此途，乃逢富贵 - Means 'All of them follow this path and thus attain wealth and honor,' describing the courtiers' motivation.
  • 陛下恶过，从何得闻 - Rhetorical question meaning 'How can Your Majesty's faults be known?' expressing the difficulty of exposing the emperor's mistakes.
  • 方今又败辽师，再幸东土 - Means 'Now, we have suffered defeat in the Liaodong campaign and the emperor has fled east again,' describing the military and political setbacks.
  • 社稷危于春雪，干戈遍于四方 - Means 'The state is in danger like spring snow, and weapons are everywhere,' emphasizing the precarious state of the nation.
  • 生民已入涂炭，官吏犹未敢言 - Means 'The people are already in the midst of chaos, and officials still dare not speak,' describing the widespread suffering and the officials' silence.
  • 陛下自惟，若何为什 - Means 'Your Majesty should reflect on what you are doing,' urging the emperor to consider his actions.
  • 陛下欲兴师则兵吏不顺，欲行幸则将卫莫从 - Means 'If Your Majesty wants to raise an army, the soldiers and officials will not obey; if You want to travel, no generals or guards will follow,' describing the lack of support for the emperor's plans.
  • 适当此时，如何自处 - Means 'At this critical time, how should You act?' questioning the emperor's response to the crisis.
  • 陛下虽欲发愤修德，特加爱民 - Means 'Even if Your Majesty wants to exert effort to cultivate virtue and show extra care for the people,' describing the emperor's potential good intentions.
  • 圣慈虽切救时，天下不可复得 - Means 'Even if Your Majesty's benevolence is urgent to save the times, the world cannot be regained,' expressing the futility of the emperor's efforts.
  • 大势已去，时不再来 - Means 'The great tide has passed, and time will not come again,' emphasizing the irreversible nature of the situation.
  • 巨厦之崩，一木不能支；洪河已决，掬壤不能救 - Means 'A great building's collapse cannot be supported by one beam; a great river's breach cannot be stopped by a handful of soil,' using metaphors to describe the overwhelming nature of the crisis.
  • 臣本远人，不知忌讳 - Means 'I am a distant person and do not know the taboos,' expressing the speaker's lack of concern for offending the emperor.
  • 事急至此，安敢不言 - Means 'The situation is urgent to this point; how dare I not speak?' expressing the speaker's resolve to speak out.
  • 臣今不死，后必死兵，敢献此书，延颈待尽 - Means 'If I do not die now, I will surely die later; I dare to present this book and wait for my death,' expressing the speaker's determination to speak out despite the risk.
  • 帝省义奏，曰：“自古安有不亡之国，不死之主乎？” - Means 'The emperor read the memorial and said, 'Since ancient times, how can there be an unvanquished kingdom or an undying ruler?'' expressing the emperor's fatalistic view.
  • 义曰：“陛下尚犹蔽饰己过。陛下常言，吾当跨三皇，超五帝，下视商周，使万世不可及。” - Means 'Yi said, 'Your Majesty still conceals Your own faults. You often say, 'I will surpass the Three Sovereigns, outdo the Five Emperors, and look down on Shang and Zhou, making it impossible for future generations to catch up.''' expressing Yi's criticism of the emperor's self-deception.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 陛下享天下之富貴 - Formal address to the emperor, meaning 'Your Majesty enjoys the wealth and nobility of the empire.'
  • 聖貌軒逸，龍章鳳姿 - Highly formal description of the emperor's appearance, meaning 'majestic and noble in demeanor, with the grace of a dragon and the beauty of a phoenix.'
  • 罔知忌諱 - Means 'without knowing any taboos' or 'without fearing to speak the truth,' suggesting that the speaker is being candid.
  • 因俯伏泣涕 - Means 'therefore, he bowed down and wept,' indicating a gesture of deep respect or sorrow.
  • 翌日，召義語之曰 - Means 'the next day, the emperor summoned Yi and said,' introducing a direct speech.
  • 朕昨夜思汝言，極有深理 - Means 'I thought about your words last night, and they are very profound,' showing the emperor's reflection on the advice.
  • 汝真爱我者也 - Means 'you truly love me,' expressing the emperor's appreciation for the speaker's honesty.
  • 乃命義後宮擇一靜室 - Means 'he then ordered Yi to choose a quiet room in the palace,' indicating a decision to isolate the emperor.
  • 忿然而出曰 - Means 'angrily left and said,' showing the emperor's frustration.
  • 安能悒悒居此乎 - Means 'how can I remain here in a depressed state?' expressing dissatisfaction with isolation.
  • 乃復入迷樓 - Means 'he then returned to the Labyrinth,' indicating a return to a place of indulgence.
  • 宮女無數 - Means 'there were countless palace maids,' emphasizing the emperor's opulence.
  • 後宮不得進御者亦極多 - Means 'there were also many in the inner palace who could not gain access,' indicating the emperor's selective favoritism.
  • 自經於棟下 - Means 'hanged herself from the eaves,' describing the method of suicide.
  • 臂懸錦囊 - Means 'with a silk bag hanging from her arm,' describing the physical state of the corpse.
  • 中有文 - Means 'containing writings,' indicating that the bag contained a written document.
  • 《自感》三首 - Means 'Three Poems of Self-Reflection,' the title of the poems found in the bag.
  • 庭絕玉輦跡，芳草漸成窠 - The first line of the poem, meaning 'The courtyard is devoid of the traces of jade carriages, and the fragrant grass gradually forms clumps,' suggesting a sense of abandonment and decay.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 承明 - The *Chengming Hall* was a place where the emperor held court, symbolizing the imperial court.
  • 未央 - The *Weiyang Palace* was a grand imperial palace in Chang'an, symbolizing the center of imperial power.
  • 长门 - The *Changmen Palace* was a palace in Chang'an, where the poet Ban Jieyu was exiled, symbolizing isolation and abandonment.
  • 侯夫人 - Lady Hou was a concubine of Emperor Yang of Sui, known for her beauty and tragic fate.
  • 迷楼 - The *Mile Tower* was a luxurious palace built by Emperor Yang of Sui, symbolizing his indulgence and excess.
  • 大业八年 - The *Daye era* was the reign period of Emperor Yang of Sui, and the eighth year of this era corresponds to 612 AD.
  • 方士 - Taoist alchemists who claimed to have the ability to produce elixirs of immortality.
  • 大丹 - A powerful elixir believed to grant longevity or immortality.
  • 真元 - Refers to the true essence or vital energy of the body, a concept in traditional Chinese medicine.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening couplet '初入承明日，深深报未央' introduces the setting and the action of the poem, but the exact meaning and context require careful interpretation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大业九年 - Refers to the ninth year of the reign of Emperor Yang of Sui (581–618 AD).
  • 江都 - Refers to Yangzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province, which was a significant cultural and economic center during the Sui and Tang dynasties.
  • 迷楼 - Refers to the Mi Lou palace, a famous pleasure palace built by Emperor Yang of Sui.
  • 河南杨柳谢，河北李花荣 - References the contrast between the withering willows in Henan and the flourishing plum blossoms in Hebei, symbolizing the decline of the Sui dynasty.
  • 杨花飞去落何处 - A metaphor for the transient nature of life and the uncertainty of fate.
  • 李花结果自然成 - A metaphor for the natural progression of events and the inevitability of outcomes.
  • 天启之也 - Refers to the belief in divine inspiration or divine will.
  • 宫中吐艳恋红辉 - A metaphor for the beauty and allure of the palace, symbolizing the emperor's indulgence and the transient nature of power.
  • 无故而歌，又悲 - A reference to the emotional state of the concubine, possibly reflecting the impending fall of the Sui dynasty.
  • 休间 - A command to the emperor's attendants to stop questioning the concubine's song.
  • 他日自知也 - A prediction of future events, hinting at the fall of the Sui dynasty.
  • 大业九年，帝将再幸江都 - Refers to Emperor Yang's plan to visit Jiangdu again, which ultimately led to his downfall.
  • 迷楼宫人静夜亢声歌云 - A reference to the concubine's song, which foreshadows the fall of the Sui dynasty.
  • 道途儿童多唱此歌 - A reference to the widespread knowledge of the song among the common people, indicating its significance.
  • 此皆民膏血所为也 - A reference to the heavy taxation and labor demands that contributed to the people's suffering and the fall of the Sui dynasty.
  • 方知世代兴亡，非偶然也 - A reference to the cyclical nature of dynastic rise and fall, emphasizing the inevitability of historical change.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '炀帝将幸江都' introduces the main action of the emperor leaving the capital and visiting Jiangdu.
  • The phrase '拜泣留帝' can be read as 'kneeling and weeping to retain the emperor,' but the exact meaning of '留帝' is somewhat ambiguous.
  • The phrase '辽东小国，不足以烦大驾' can be interpreted as 'Liaodong is a small country and not worth troubling the emperor’s presence,' but the exact nuance is open to interpretation.
  • The phrase '攀车留籍指血染鞅' is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of actions and the emotional state of the women.
  • The phrase '因戏飞白题二十字' suggests that the writing was done as a joke, but the exact context and tone are not fully clear.
  • The phrase '我梦江都好，征辽亦偶然' can be read as 'dreaming that Jiangdu was good, the campaign against Liaodong was also accidental,' but the exact meaning of '偶然' (accidental) is open to interpretation.
  • The phrase '但存颜色在，离别只今年' can be read as 'as long as your beauty remains, our parting is only for this year,' but the exact emotional tone is not fully clear.
  • The phrase '车驾既行，师徒百万' introduces the scale of the emperor’s entourage and the military forces accompanying him.
  • The phrase '前趋大桥未就，命云屯将军麻叔谋浚黄河人汴堤' introduces the action of advancing towards the great bridge not yet completed and ordering the Yún Tún General Má Shūmóu to dredge the Yellow River and enter the Bian River embankment.

  • 嗅之令人不多睡 - The fragrance is so pleasant that it makes one less sleepy.
  • 帝令宝儿持之，号日司花女 - The emperor had Yuan Baoer hold the flower and called her 'Si Hua Nu' (司花女), the Flower Lady.
  • 时诏虞世南草征辽指挥德音敕于帝侧 - The emperor ordered Yu Shina to draft a decree about the campaign against Liaodong while standing beside the emperor.
  • 宝儿注视久之 - Yuan Baoer stared at Yu Shina for a long time.
  • 昔传飞燕可掌上舞 - Refers to the legend of the Flying Swallow who could dance on her fingertips.
  • 儒生饰于文字，岂人能若是乎 - Questions whether scholars exaggerate in their writings, implying that such feats are unlikely.
  • 及今得宝儿，方昭前事 - Now that Yuan Baoer is here, it shows that such things are possible.
  • 然多憨态 - But she is clumsy.
  • 今注目于卿，卿才人，可便嘲之 - The emperor asks Yu Shina, a talented man, to mock her.
  • 世南应诏为绝句曰 - Yu Shina responds with a poem.

  • 嵩山坞中 - A location on Mount Song, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.

  • 虞世南 - A famous scholar and poet during the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary talent and political influence.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 學畫鸦黄半未成 - Phrase describing someone learning to paint with yellow pigment, possibly incomplete or in progress.
  • 垂肩袖太憨生 - Describes a sleeve hanging down to the shoulders, suggesting a naive or innocent appearance.
  • 緣憨却得君王惜 - The emperor favored the woman due to her innocence.
  • 常把花枝傍辇行 - The woman often held flowers while walking beside the imperial carriage.
  • 上大悦 - The emperor was greatly pleased.
  • 至汴，帝御龙舟 - The emperor arrived at Bian and rode in the dragon boat.
  • 萧妃乘凤舸 - Empress Xiao rode in the phoenix boat.
  • 锦帆彩缆，穷极侈靡 - Describes the luxurious sails and ropes, indicating extreme extravagance.
  • 舟前为舞台，台上垂蔽日帘 - The boat had a stage at the front with a sun-blocking curtain.
  • 虽晓日激射，而光不能透 - Even though the morning sun was shining brightly, the light could not penetrate the curtain.
  • 每舟择妙丽长白女子千人，执雕板镂金揖，号为殿脚女 - Each boat selected a thousand beautiful, fair-skinned women to hold intricately carved and gold-inlaid paddles, called 'palace foot girls.'
  • 一日，帝将登凤舸，凭殿脚女吴练仙肩，喜其柔丽，不与群辈齿 - One day, the emperor was about to board the phoenix boat and leaned on the shoulder of a palace foot girl named Wu Lianxian, liking her soft beauty and not ranking her with the others.
  • 绛仙善画长蛾眉，帝色不自禁 - Jiang Xian was skilled at painting long, arched eyebrows, and the emperor could not control his desire.
  • 回辇，召绛仙，将拜捷好 - The emperor returned to the carriage and summoned Jiang Xian, about to bestow a favor.
  • 适值绛仙下嫁为玉工万群妻，故不克谐 - Just as Jiang Xian had married a jade craftsman named Wan Qun, the plan could not be carried out.
  • 帝寝兴罢，擢为龙舟首揖，号曰崆峒夫人 - After the emperor had rested, he promoted her to the position of chief paddle of the dragon boat and called her 'Lady Kungtung.'
  • 由是，殿脚女争效为长蛾眉 - From then on, the palace foot girls competed to paint long, arched eyebrows.
  • 司官吏日给螺子黛五斛，号为蛾绿 - The officials daily provided five measures of shell powder, which was called 'moth green.'
  • 螺子黛，出波斯国，每颗值十金 - Shell powder came from Persia, each bead was worth ten gold pieces.
  • 后征赋不足，杂以铜黛给之 - Later, when the taxes were insufficient, they mixed it with copper powder and gave it to them.
  • 独绛仙得赐螺黛不绝 - Only Jiang Xian continued to receive the shell powder.
  • 帝每倚帘视绛仙，移时不去 - The emperor often leaned on the curtain to look at Jiang Xian and did not leave for a long time.
  • 因吟诗揖篇赐之曰 - He then recited a poem and presented it to her.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 旧曲歌桃叶 - Refers to an old song about peach leaves, likely a metaphorical or symbolic reference in the context of the poem.
  • 新妆艳落梅 - Describes a new makeup style that is as beautiful as falling plum blossoms.
  • 将身旁轻楫 - Means 'with a light boat by her side,' possibly symbolizing ease or grace.
  • 知是渡江来 - Means 'knowing she has come across the river,' suggesting arrival or transition.
  • 诏殿脚女千辈唱之 - Means 'thousands of palace attendants were ordered to sing it,' indicating a grand performance.
  • 越溪进耀光绫 - Refers to a bright, lustrous silk from the Yue River, emphasizing its quality and origin.
  • 绫纹突起有光彩 - Describes the silk's texture as having raised patterns with a shimmering effect.
  • 越人乘樵风舟，泛于石帆山下 - Describes the people from Yue traveling on a boat powered by the 'chopping wind' (a poetic term for a gentle breeze) near Stone Sail Mountain.
  • 收野茧缫之 - Means 'collecting wild silkworm cocoons and spinning them into silk,' highlighting the process of silk production.
  • 缫丝女夜梦神人告之曰 - Describes a dream where a silk spinner is told by a divine figure.
  • 禹穴三千年一开 - Refers to the legendary tomb of Yu the Great, which is said to open every three thousand years, symbolizing rarity or divine intervention.
  • 汝所得野茧，即江淹文集中壁鱼所化也 - Means 'the wild silk you have obtained is transformed from the wall fish in Jiang Yan's literary collection,' a reference to a literary legend.
  • 丝织为裳，必有奇文 - Means 'if woven into clothing, it will surely have extraordinary patterns,' suggesting the silk's magical or special qualities.
  • 帝独赐司花女洎绛仙 - Means 'the emperor bestowed the silk exclusively on the Flower Girl and the Red Immortal,' indicating their favored status.
  • 他姬莫预 - Means 'other concubines were not included,' emphasizing the exclusivity of the gift.
  • 萧妃恚妒不怿 - Describes Xiao Fei's jealousy and displeasure.
  • 由是二姬稍稍不得亲幸 - Means 'as a result, the two girls gradually lost favor,' indicating a decline in their status.
  • 帝尝醉游诸宫，偶戏官婢罗罗者 - Describes the emperor, drunk, playing with the palace maid Luo Luo.
  • 罗罗畏萧妃，不敢迎帝，且辞以有程姬之疾，不可荐寝 - Means 'Luo Luo, fearing Xiao Fei, did not welcome the emperor and excused herself by saying she had the illness of Cheng Ji, making it impossible for her to sleep with him,' referring to a historical figure who was ill and thus unavailable.
  • 帝乃嘲之曰 - Means 'the emperor then mocked her,' setting up a humorous or sarcastic response.

  • 诏殿脚女千辈唱之 - Refers to a grand performance in the imperial court, emphasizing the importance and popularity of the song.
  • 越溪 - The Yue River, a significant geographical feature in ancient China.
  • 耀光绫 - A type of high-quality silk, emphasizing its brightness and luster.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 个人无赖是横波 - Describes someone who is free-spirited or unrestrained.
  • 黛染隆颅簇小蛾 - Describes a person with a high forehead and eyebrows that resemble clustered small moths.
  • 幸得留侬伴成梦 - Expresses the idea of being fortunate to be kept in someone's company in a dream.
  • 不留侬住意如何 - Asks about the implications or feelings of not being kept in someone's company.
  • 帝自达广陵宫中，多效吴言，因有侬语也 - Explains that the emperor often imitated the Wu dialect, leading to the use of '侬' in his speech.
  • 帝昏湎滋深，往往为妖祟所惑 - Describes the emperor's increasing indulgence and susceptibility to evil spirits.
  • 尝游吴公宅鸡台，恍惚间与陈后主相遇 - Describes the emperor's visit to a place where he encountered Emperor Chen in a dream-like state.
  • 尚唤帝为殿下 - Indicates that Emperor Chen still addressed the emperor with respect.
  • 后主戴车纱皂帻，青绰袖，长裾，绿锦纯缘紫纹，方平履 - Describes Emperor Chen's attire.
  • 舞女数十许，罗侍左右 - Describes the arrangement of dancing girls around Emperor Chen.
  • 中一女迥美，帝屡目之 - Describes the emperor's attention to a particularly beautiful woman.
  • 每忆桃叶山前，乘战舰与此子北渡 - Describes Emperor Chen's memories of a past event.
  • 尔时丽华最恨，方倚临春阁，试东郭紫毫笔，书小砑红绡作答江令壁月句 - Describes Li Hua's actions during that time.
  • 未终，见韩擒虎跃青骢驹，拥万甲直来冲人，都不存去就 - Describes the sudden appearance of Han Qintiger and his troops.
  • 至今日 - Indicates that the events described are relevant to the present.
  • 俄以绿文测海蠡，酌红粱新酿劝帝 - Describes Emperor Chen's act of offering wine to the emperor.
  • 帝饮之，甚欢 - Describes the emperor's reaction to the wine.
  • 因请丽华舞玉树后庭花 - Describes the emperor's request for Li Hua to perform a dance.
  • 丽华白后主，辞以抛掷岁久，自井中出来，腰肢依巨，元复往时姿态 - Describes Li Hua's explanation for not dancing.
  • 帝再三索之，乃徐起终一曲 - Describes the emperor's persistence and Li Hua's eventual performance.
  • 后主问帝：“萧妃何如此人？” - Emperor Chen's question to the emperor.
  • 帝曰：“春兰秋菊，各一时之秀也。” - The emperor's response, comparing Xiao Fei and the woman.
  • 后主复诵诗十数篇，帝不记之。独爱小窗诗及寄侍儿碧玉诗 - Describes the emperor's preference for certain poems.
  • 小窗诗云 - Introduces the content of the poem.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 广陵宫 - Refers to the Guangling Palace, a place associated with the emperor's visit.
  • 吴言 - Refers to the Wu dialect, a regional dialect spoken in the Wu region.
  • 侬 - A term of endearment or informal address, commonly used in the Wu dialect.
  • 陈后主 - Refers to Emperor Chen of the Chen dynasty, a historical figure known for his indulgence and the fall of the Chen dynasty.
  • 桃叶山 - Refers to Taoye Mountain, a place associated with the emperor's memories.

[348]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 帝幸月观 - The emperor visited the Moon Pavilion, where '幸' means 'to visit' or 'to grace with a presence.'
  • 烟景清朗 - The smoke-filled scenery was clear and bright, a poetic description of the scene.
  • 中夜 - Midnight, indicating the time of the event.
  • 帘栊不开 - The curtains and window grilles were not opened, suggesting privacy or seclusion.
  • 左右方寝 - The attendants were just sleeping, indicating that the surroundings were quiet.
  • 帝凭妃肩说东宫时事 - The emperor leaned on the shoulder of the concubine and talked about matters from the Eastern Palace, where '东宫' refers to the residence of the crown prince.
  • 小黄门映蔷蔽丛调宫婢 - A small eunuch was peeking through the rose bushes and teasing a palace maid, where '小黄门' refers to a low-ranking eunuch, and '蔷蔽丛' means 'rose bushes.'
  • 衣带为蔷蔽结 - Her sash got caught in the rose bushes, indicating a physical entanglement.
  • 笑声哧哧不止 - Her laughter kept going on and on, describing the maid's giggles.
  • 帝望见腰肢纤弱 - The emperor saw her slender waist, indicating his observation of the maid.
  • 意为宝儿有私 - He thought that the maid had a secret affair, where '宝儿' is a term of endearment for the maid.
  • 帝披单衣，亟行擒之 - The emperor put on a robe and hurriedly went to catch her, indicating his swift action.
  • 乃宫婢雅娘也 - It turned out to be the palace maid named Yan娘, identifying the maid.
  • 回入寝殿，萧妃俏笑不知止 - He returned to the sleeping chamber, and Xiao's Consort laughed sweetly without stopping, indicating her amusement.
  • 往年私幸妥娘时，情态正如此 - When I secretly favored Consort Tu years ago, she behaved just like this, referring to a past relationship.
  • 此时虽有性命，不复惜矣 - At that time, even if I lost my life, I wouldn't care anymore, expressing a sense of abandon.
  • 后得月宾，被伊作意态不彻，是时侬怜心不减今日对萧娘情态 - Later, when I got Consort Yuebin, she always acted coy, and at that time, my affection for her was no less than my feelings for Xiao's Consort today, comparing past and present affections.
  • 曾效刘孝绰为杂忆诗，常念与妃，妃记之否 - I once wrote a miscellaneous memory poem in the style of Liu Xiaochuo, and I often recited it to the consort. Do you remember it? indicating a shared memory.
  • 忆睡时，待来刚不来 - Remember when we were sleeping, waiting for someone who didn't come, describing a moment of anticipation.
  • 卸妆仍索伴，解佩更相催 - After removing makeup, I still wanted company, and after unfastening the jade pendant, we urged each other, indicating a playful interaction.
  • 博山思结梦，沉水未成灰 - I dreamed of the incense burner, and the incense was still burning, symbolizing lingering thoughts.
  • 忆起时，投签初报晓 - Remember when we woke up, the timekeeper's rod just announced the dawn, describing a moment of waking.
  • 被惹香黛残，枕隐金钗袅 - The fragrance of the face powder was still lingering, and the gold hairpin was hidden in the pillow, indicating a sense of lingering presence.
  • 笑动上林中，除却司晨鸟 - Laughter echoed in the imperial garden, except for the bird that announces the morning, suggesting a joyful atmosphere.
  • 日月遄逝，今来已是几年事矣 - Time flies, and it's already been several years since then, expressing the passage of time.
  • 闻说方外群盗不少，幸帝图之 - I heard that there are many bandits outside the borders, may the emperor take action against them, suggesting a concern for external threats.
  • 侬家事，一切已托杨素了，人生能几何，纵有他变，侬终不失作长城公 - All matters of the household have been entrusted to Yang Su, life is short, even if there are other changes, I will never lose my status as the Great Wall Prince, indicating a sense of resignation and trust in others.

[349]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汝无言外事也 - Direct command meaning 'Do not speak of matters outside [your] duties.'
  • 帝尝幸昭明文选楼 - The emperor once visited the Zhaoming Wenxuan Pavilion. *幸* indicates a royal visit, which carries a connotation of favor or grace.
  • 车驾未至，先命宫娥数千升楼迎侍 - Before the imperial carriage arrived, he ordered thousands of palace maids to ascend the pavilion to wait on him. *升楼* means 'to ascend the pavilion,' and *迎侍* means 'to wait on him.'
  • 微风东来，宫娥衣被风绰，直泊肩项 - A gentle east wind blew, and the palace maids' clothes were fluttering in the breeze, reaching their shoulders and necks. *衣被风绰* describes the fluttering of the clothes, and *直泊肩项* means 'reaching their shoulders and necks.'
  • 帝睹之色荒愈炽 - The emperor, upon seeing this, became even more lustful. *色荒* is a euphemism for sexual desire, and *愈炽* means 'even more intense.'
  • 因此乃建迷楼 - As a result, he built the Labyrinth Pavilion. *迷楼* refers to a complex and confusing structure, often used metaphorically for a place of debauchery.
  • 择下俚稚女居之，使衣轻罗单裳，倚槛望之势若飞举 - He chose young girls from the lower classes to live there, dressing them in light silk and single-layered garments, so that when they leaned against the railings, they appeared as if they were flying. *下俚稚女* means 'young girls from the lower classes,' *轻罗单裳* refers to light silk and single-layered garments, and *倚槛望之势若飞举* describes the appearance of the girls leaning against the railings.
  • 又名香于四隅，烟气霏霏常若朝雾未散，谓为神仙境不我多也 - He also had incense burned in the four corners, and the smoke drifted like morning mist, creating an atmosphere that was called a celestial realm, not unlike a paradise. *名香于四隅* means 'burning incense in the four corners,' *烟气霏霏常若朝雾未散* describes the drifting smoke, and *谓为神仙境不我多也* means 'it was called a celestial realm, not unlike a paradise.'
  • 楼上张四宝帐，帐各异名，一名散春愁，二名醉忘归，三名夜酣香，四名延秋月 - On the upper floor, he had four precious canopies hung, each with a different name: one called 'Dispelling Spring Sorrow,' another 'Drunk and Forgetful of Returning,' the third 'Nightly Intoxication and Fragrance,' and the fourth 'Extending the Autumn Moon.' *张四宝帐* means 'hung four precious canopies,' and *帐各异名* means 'each with a different name.'
  • 妆奁、寝衣、帐各异制 - The makeup boxes, sleeping clothes, and canopies were all made in different styles. *妆奁* refers to makeup boxes, *寝衣* means sleeping clothes, and *帐各异制* means 'all made in different styles.'
  • 帝自达广陵，沉缅失度，每睡，须摇顿四体，或歌吹齐鼓，方就一梦 - The emperor, after reaching Guangling, became deeply immersed in pleasure, losing all sense of moderation. Every time he slept, he had to shake his body and sometimes sing and play music, before he could fall asleep. *沉缅失度* means 'deeply immersed in pleasure, losing all sense of moderation,' *摇顿四体* means 'shaking his body,' and *歌吹齐鼓* means 'singing and playing music.'
  • 侍儿韩俊娥，尤得帝意 - The servant girl Han Jun'e especially pleased the emperor. *尤得帝意* means 'especially pleased the emperor.'
  • 每寝必召令振耸支节，然后成寝 - Every time he slept, he would call her to make her limbs stiffen, and only then could he fall asleep. *振耸支节* means 'make her limbs stiffen.'
  • 别赐名为来梦儿 - He gave her a special name, 'Dream-Bringer.' *来梦儿* means 'Dream-Bringer.'
  • 萧妃常密讯俊娥曰：“帝体不舒，汝能安之，岂有他媚？” - Empress Xiao often secretly questioned Han Jun'e, saying, 'The emperor's body is not comfortable; you can soothe him, but do you have any other charms?' *密讯* means 'secretly questioned,' *体不舒* means 'body is not comfortable,' *安之* means 'soothe him,' and *他媚* means 'other charms.'
  • 他日，萧后诬罪去之，帝不能止 - One day, Empress Xiao falsely accused her of a crime and had her removed, and the emperor could not stop it. *诬罪去之* means 'falsely accused her of a crime and had her removed.'
  • 暇日登迷楼忆之，题东南柱二篇云 - On a leisurely day, the emperor climbed the Labyrinth Pavilion and remembered her, writing two poems on the southeast pillar. *暇日* means 'leisurely day,' *登迷楼忆之* means 'climbed the Labyrinth Pavilion and remembered her,' and *题东南柱二篇云* means 'wrote two poems on the southeast pillar.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汝无言外事也 - A command to stay within one's duties, reflecting the hierarchical nature of the imperial court.
  • 帝尝幸昭明文选楼 - The Zhaoming Wenxuan Pavilion is a famous literary site associated with the Liang dynasty, known for its collection of literary works.
  • 车驾未至，先命宫娥数千升楼迎侍 - The practice of having palace maids wait on the emperor reflects the elaborate rituals and protocols of the imperial court.
  • 微风东来，宫娥衣被风绰，直泊肩项 - The description of the palace maids' attire and the gentle wind evokes a scene of elegance and grace, typical of courtly life.
  • 帝睹之色荒愈炽 - The term *色荒* is a euphemism for sexual desire, reflecting the emperor's indulgence in pleasure.
  • 因此乃建迷楼 - The Labyrinth Pavilion is a metaphor for a place of debauchery, often associated with the emperor's excessive indulgence.
  • 择下俚稚女居之，使衣轻罗单裳，倚槛望之势若飞举 - The choice of young girls from the lower classes and their attire reflects the emperor's disregard for social norms and his desire for pleasure.
  • 又名香于四隅，烟气霏霏常若朝雾未散，谓为神仙境不我多也 - The use of incense and the creation of a mystical atmosphere reflect the emperor's desire to create a paradise-like setting.
  • 楼上张四宝帐，帐各异名，一名散春愁，二名醉忘归，三名夜酣香，四名延秋月 - The four canopies with different names reflect the emperor's desire to create a luxurious and pleasurable environment.
  • 妆奁、寝衣、帐各异制 - The variety of styles in the makeup boxes, sleeping clothes, and canopies reflects the emperor's attention to detail and his desire for luxury.
  • 帝自达广陵，沉缅失度，每睡，须摇顿四体，或歌吹齐鼓，方就一梦 - The emperor's behavior in Guangling reflects his excessive indulgence and loss of moderation.

  • 宁知辞帝里，无复合欢心 - Expresses the sorrow of being separated from the imperial palace and the inability to find happiness again.

  • 離合之意 - The idea of combining or separating parts of characters to create new meanings.
  • 移左畫居右 - A specific method of rearranging strokes within a character to form a new one.
  • 非聖人耶 - A rhetorical question implying that the emperor is questioning whether someone is a sage or not.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, likely Emperor Yang of Sui (Yang Guang).
  • 蕭妃 - Refers to Xiao Yanyan, the empress of Emperor Yang of Sui.
  • 唐公 - Refers to Li Yuan, the founder of the Tang Dynasty, who was a prominent figure during Emperor Yang's reign.
  • 直閣斐虔通、虎赍郎将司马德勤等 - Names of officials and military leaders who were involved in the plot against the emperor.
  • 字文化及 - A high-ranking official and military leader who played a key role in the rebellion.
  • 門下 - Refers to the gate of the palace, often used in official decrees to address the officials.
  • 寒暑迭用 - A metaphor for the natural cycles of the seasons, often used to describe the balance of power or the rotation of officials.
  • 日月代明 - A metaphor for the cyclical nature of leadership and governance.
  • 士子有游息之谈 - Refers to the idea that scholars and officials should have periods of rest and leisure.
  • 农夫有休劳之节 - Refers to the idea that farmers should have periods of rest and labor.
  • 埃溢于爪发 - Describes the dirt and grime on the hands and hair, indicating hard labor.
  • 虮虱结于兜鍪 - Describes lice and other pests in the helmet, indicating the harsh conditions of military service.
  • 俾尔休番 - Means 'to allow you to take turns resting,' a reference to the rotation of duties.
  • 无烦方朔滑稽之请 - Refers to the idea of not needing the humorous and satirical advice of Sima Qian (a historical figure known for his wit and humor).
  • 卫士递上之文 - Refers to the idea of allowing guards to take turns on duty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 右《大业拾遗记》者 - Formal introduction indicating the text is titled 'The Supplement to the Great Cause'.
  • 上元县南朝故都 - Refers to the ancient capital of the Southern Dynasties located in Shangyuan County.
  • 梁建瓦棺寺阁 - Indicates that during the Liang Dynasty, a pagoda was built at the Wuguan Temple.
  • 阁南隅有双阁 - Describes a pair of towers located in the southern corner of the pagoda.
  • 闭之忘记岁月 - Means that the towers were sealed away and forgotten over time.
  • 会昌中，诏拆浮图 - During the Huichang period, an imperial edict was issued to dismantle the pagoda.
  • 因开之 - As a result, the sealed towers were opened.
  • 得笔千余头 - Revealed over a thousand writing implements.
  • 中藏书一帙 - Inside, a bundle of books was found.
  • 虽皆随手靡书 - Although most of the books were worn out by handling.
  • 而文字可纪者 - There were still some texts that could be recorded.
  • 乃隋书遗稿也 - These were drafts of books from the Sui Dynasty.
  • 中有生白藤纸数幅 - Among them were several sheets of living white vine paper.
  • 题为南部烟花录 - Labeled as 'Southern Floral and Lantern Records'.
  • 僧志彻得之 - A monk named Zhichi obtained it.
  • 及焚释氏群经 - When the Buddhist scriptures were burned.
  • 僧人惜其香轴 - The monks valued the incense sticks attached to the scrolls.
  • 争取纸尾拆去 - They tore off the ends of the scrolls to take the incense sticks.
  • 视轴 - Upon examining the scrolls.
  • 皆有鲁郡文中颜公名题云手写 - All had inscriptions by Yan Gong of Luzheng Prefecture, stating they were handwritten.
  • 是录即前之笔 - This record is the same as the previously mentioned writing implements.
  • 可不举而知也 - It is clear without needing to mention it further.
  • 志彻得录前事 - Monk Zhichi recorded the preceding events.
  • 及取隋书校之 - When comparing it with the Sui Dynasty books.
  • 多隐文 - Many hidden texts were found.
  • 特有符会 - There were special correspondences.
  • 而事颇简脱 - But the accounts were somewhat simplified.
  • 岂不以国初将相争以王道辅政 - Is it not because the early statesmen competed to support governance with the Way of Kings?
  • 颜公不欲华靡前迹 - Yan Gong did not want to glorify past splendor.
  • 因而削乎 - Therefore, he cut it out.
  • 今尧风已还 - Now the virtue of Yao has returned.
  • 得车斯驾 - The right person is in the right place.
  • 独惜斯文湮没 - Only regretting that this literature has been lost.
  • 不得为词人才子谈柄 - Unable to serve as a topic for poets and talented scholars.

  • 悉而补之矣 - All have been supplemented.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 词人才子 - Refers to poets and talented scholars, indicating the cultural elite.
